coredata.dc:title: A facile green synthesis of functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for a highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Latief, Urosa
ul Islam, Shafi
Khan, Zubair M.S.H.
Khan, Mohd. Shahid
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have reported an economical, easy, greener and non-toxic synthesis route of water soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through hydrothermal treatment using gelatin as precursor. Under the UV lamp of wavelength 365 nm, the as-prepared CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence along with CIE coordinate index of (0.17, 0.14) and possess a quantum yield of 22.7% with rhodamine B as standard. The morphology of as-synthesized CQDs as investigated by TEM measurement confirmed their spherical shape and also revealed that their sizes varied in the scale of 0.5–5 nm. Furthermore, the CQDs showed excitation dependent fluorescence emission behaviour in range of 280 nm to 420 nm as a result of quantum confinement effect. Apart from this, in CQDs solution, the addition of Fe3+ ion lead to fluorescence quenching effect. These results revealed that the as-synthesized CQDs have a sensitive response towards the Fe3+ ion. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 0.2 μM with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996 in the concentration range 0 to 50 μM. More remarkably, the application of CQDs for monitoring the trace level of Fe3+ ion in tap water yielded acceptable recoveries (103.33%-105%). Therefore, this work provides a novel additional fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ion in real world.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Quantum yield
Fe3+ ion detection
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and surface study of CQDs
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful to Central Instrumental Facility (CIF), Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi for providing research infrastructure for various characterization facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM micrograph at scale bar 20 nm of as-synthesized CQDs whereas inset shows the particle size histogram of CQDs (b) TEM micrograph at scale bar 5 nm of as-synthesized CQDs whereas inset shows the SAED pattern of CQD (c) XRD pattern of as-synthesized CQDs (d) FTIR spectrum of as-synthesized CQDs.
(a) Complete XPS survey spectra of as-synthesized CQDs (b) C1s XPS spectrum (c) N1s XPS spectrum (d) O1s XPS spectrum.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CQDs; inset shows the image of as-synthesized CQDs under the UV lamp and under normal light (b) CIE co-ordinates of as-synthesized CQDs exhibiting blue fluorescence emission (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of as-synthesized CQDs at different excitation wavelength ranging from the 280 to 420 nm (d) Fluorescence life time decay of CQDs with and without Fe3+concentration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission intensity of as-synthesized CQDs as a function of different ionic concentration ranging from 0 to 2 M (b) Fluorescence emission intensity of as-synthesized CQDs as a function of different pH values ranging from 3 to 10 (c) influence of distinct incubation time on the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity after incorporation of different metal ions of as-synthesized CQDs (b) fluorescence quenching response after incorporation of different concentration of Fe3+ of as-synthesized CQDs ranging from 0 to 500 μM. Error bars represent the three replicate measurements.
Schematic diagram exhibiting the quenching mechanism on the addition of Fe3+ ions of as-synthesized CQDs.

(a) Relationship curve between FL emission intensity as a function of different concentration of Fe3+ (b) a linear correlation between FL emission intensity variation versus Fe3+concentration in the scale of 0–50 μM. Error bars represent the three replicate measurements.
Schematic diagram of blue fluorescent CQDs synthesized through one step hydrothermal treatment.
Comparison of synthesis method, size, optical properties and quantum yield with the earlier literature report.

row.entry: N, Fe co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test in tap water (n=3) for the detection of spiked Fe3+ measured with as-synthesized CQDs.

head.ce:title: A facile green synthesis of functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for a highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have reported an economical, easy, greener and non-toxic synthesis route of water soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through hydrothermal treatment using gelatin as precursor. Under the UV lamp of wavelength 365 nm, the as-prepared CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence along with CIE coordinate index of (0.17, 0.14) and possess a quantum yield of 22.7% with rhodamine B as standard. The morphology of as-synthesized CQDs as investigated by TEM measurement confirmed their spherical shape and also revealed that their sizes varied in the scale of 0.5–5 nm. Furthermore, the CQDs showed excitation dependent fluorescence emission behaviour in range of 280 nm to 420 nm as a result of quantum confinement effect. Apart from this, in CQDs solution, the addition of Fe3+ ion lead to fluorescence quenching effect. These results revealed that the as-synthesized CQDs have a sensitive response towards the Fe3+ ion. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 0.2 μM with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996 in the concentration range 0 to 50 μM. More remarkably, the application of CQDs for monitoring the trace level of Fe3+ ion in tap water yielded acceptable recoveries (103.33%-105%). Therefore, this work provides a novel additional fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ion in real world.

ce:section.ce:para: By virtue of the perception of the importance of several transition and heavy metal ions in natural, biological and chemical processes, artificial chemosensors which are effective for sensing transition metal ions have acquired substantial attention in the past decades [1]. Out of these transition metals, iron is the most versatile and abundant transition metal and plays a crucial role in a variety of physical and biological systems [2,3] due to its high affinity for oxygen and facile redox chemistry [4,5]. Therefore, in order to develop a sensitive and selective method to analyse Fe3+ ion in environmental and biological processes, extreme research effort should be made. For an analysis of Fe3+, there exist many methods like electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [6], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], plasma coupled spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [8], mass spectroscopy [9] and electrochemistry [10]. However, these methods still have many short comings, for example, pre-sampling treatment, lengthy operation procedure, and the manipulation of sophisticated instrumentations [11]. Thus, to establish a manageable, convenient and fast method for determining Fe3+ ion is of great significance. Fluorescent chemosensors have several benefits like high sensitivity, high selectivity, non-costly, easy to operate and real time monitoring with fast response. Though, semiconductor quantum dots fluorescent probes such as CdSe quantum dots, CdTe quantum dots, etc. detect heavy metals but they are not conducive in biological systems for safe detection [12,13]. Therefore, we developed a novel quantum dots based fluorescent sensor which is metal free with exceptional biocompatibility. Scheme 1.

In light of new category of nanomaterials based on carbon, CQDs have been highlighted as an efficient candidate for fluorescent sensor for the detection of biomolecules and small molecules [14,15], metal ions [16-19] due to their excellent material properties like excellent biocompatibility, great hydrophilicity, efficient photoluminescence, a low cost fabrication and unique chemical inertness [20-24]. Various studies have reported different paths for the synthesis of CQDs; like microwave heating [25], electrochemical oxidation [26], combustion [27], laser ablation [28], plasma treatment [29], arc discharge [30] and hydrothermal method [31,32]. Inspite of these tremendous advancements, there is demand for the reliable and eco-friendly synthesis of high quality CQDs. Nowadays, the water based soft synthesis pathway, hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs came out to be simple and cost effective due to good selectivity, simple manipulation, low energy consumption and a product synthesis without any complicated control in one step process [33]. There are various studies available for the green synthesis of CQDs. For example, Trong et al. [34] presented easy and nature friendly method by making use of banana peel as a precursor to acquire highly fluorescent CQDs. Other study reported the synthesis of CQDs by using the hydrothermal process from grass with only 6.2% of quantum yield [35]. Navneet et al. [36]. demonstrated green synthesis approach of CQDs and applied as fluorescent probes for a detection of Cu2+ ion based on hydrothermal strategy using banana with LOD 0.3 μgml−1 in the concentration range of 1–800 μg/ml. Zhang et al. [37] prepared CQDs from ginkgo leaves for bioimaging and a detection of Hg2+ in the range of 0.50–20 μM having LOD 12.4 nM with a quantum yield of 11%. Sabet et al. [38] documented the green synthesis of nitrogen doped CQDs from grass and used them for eliminating both organic and inorganic pollutions from drinking water. Various organic compounds such as citric acid, glycerol, chitosan, polybasic acid and urea have been used for the preparation of CQDs [39]. But these organic compounds are restricted because of surface passivation, strict reaction condition and toxicity. Thus, the selection of an appropriate precursor is an important factor that should be taken under consideration. Hence it is necessary to find some green, cheap and source carbon materials as carbon is abundant in nature which make it cheaper and easier to be used and grant researchers a privilege to grow high quality CQDs. Recently, Hsu and Chang observed that the material containing both amino and carboxyl group have a higher photoluminescence quantum yield and are useful for the synthesis of CQDs [40]. With this scenario, we proposed that gelatin could be a good source for the synthesis of CQDs as it contains both amino and carboxyl group. Although there are numerous reports available for the synthesis of CQDs using gelatin as a precursor [41,42] but their utilization as fluorescent sensor towards the detection of Fe3+ ion has not been reported yet.

Here we reported the synthesis of nitrogen rich CQDs using low cost gelatin as precursor by employing hydrothermal approach. The investigation of fluorescent properties of as-synthesized CQDs and their use as fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ion via quenching mechanism have been carried out systematically and are reported in the following sections.

In our work, analytical grade chemicals were used. Gelatin was purchased from Chemical drug house Ltd. India and used without any further purification. The metal salts CdCl2, Zn(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2·10H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Bi(NO3)2·5H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, FeN3O9·9H2O were purchased from the Alfa Aesar. Sr(NO3)2, SnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O were supplied by Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. India. NaCl, NaOH, CH3COOH and CH3COONa were purchased from S D Fine Chem Ltd. India. Throughout the whole experiment, deionized water was used.

Typically, 0.72 g of gelatin was taken and poured into 36 ml of de ionized water in a small beaker. The obtained solution was heated at 40 °C on stirrer for 30 min. Consequently, the resulting solution was transferred into stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner of 60 ml capacity in hydrothermal and was put in a furnace for a time period of 3 h at a temperature of 200 °C. After that, the reactor was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The resulting light yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm up to 30 min to remove foreign particles and a supernatant solution was collected. Further, this solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter giving a light brown aqueous solution of CQDs which was mixed with ultrapure water for further use.

The as-synthesized CQDs were investigated through various characterization techniques. The shape and size were recorded on Transmission Electron Microscope using JEOL JEM-F200 at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction data were measured from Rigaku smart lab with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54059 Å). By using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum100), various functional groups were investigated. X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy was carried out to explore the bonding and chemical composition existing on the surface using Versa Probe III (XPS) Surface Analysis Instrument. LABMAN Lcd LMSP-UV1900S double beam UV–Vis Spectrophotometer was used to investigate the optical properties. All fluorescence spectra were measured using Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer. Further, Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup FL920 from Edinburgh Instruments, UK was used for fluorescence decay measurement.

By taking rhodamine B in water (QY = 0.31) as reference, quantum yield of the as-synthesized CQDs was estimated. The quantum yield of CQDs in water was calculated according to Eq. (1) [36]
(1)
Φ
x
=
Φ
r
.
I
x
I
r
.
A
r
A
x
.
η
x
2
η
r
2

Where the subscripts “x” and “r” are assigned for CQDs and rhodamine B respectively. “Ф” is fluorescent quantum yield. “I” designates the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, “A” denotes the absorbance measured at 340 nm and “η” is the refractive index of the solution in distilled water.

The turn-off detection of Fe3+ was accomplished in sodium acetate buffer (Na-Ac) at room temperature (0.2 M, pH 7). Generally, solution is formed by mixing 1 ml of Na-Ac buffer with 50 μL of the as-synthesized CQDs and then different metals ion solution concentration were added accompanied by the incorporation of different concentrations of Fe3+. Also, by adding deionized water, the resulting volume was increased up to 3 ml. After 5 min of incubation time, the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra of the reaction were observed. Eventually, the stability, selectivity and sensitivity of as-synthesized nano fluorescent sensor were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface study of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized at 200 °C through one step hydrothermal treatment of gelatin for 3 h as described in Schematic 1. The size distribution and surface structure of as-synthesized CQDs was carried out by TEM characterization technique. Fig. 1 (a) shows TEM micrograph of the as-synthesized CQDs at scale bar 20 nm from which the abundance of small sized and spherical shaped CQDs having maximum size distribution in the range of 2–2.5 nm can be seen. Fig. 1(b) shows TEM micrograph of the as-synthesized CQDs at scale bar 5 nm where the inset depicts the SAED micrograph showing a broad circular ring which confirms the amorphous nature of the as-synthesized CQDs. Also, X-ray diffraction measurement were carried out for structural analysis which revealed that the as-prepared CQDs exhibited amorphous nature as shown in Fig. 1(c). The broad peak at 2θ = 23.80 corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite [43,44]. These results confirmed that the as-synthesized CQDs exhibited both amorphous and graphitic nature.

In order to study the surface groups bonded on CQDs, FTIR spectrum was recorded. Fig. 1(d) shows that a wide absorption band at 3281 cm−1 is observed and is assigned to the stretching vibrations N-H and O-H [45]. A sharp peak is observed at 1634 cm−1 which indicates the presence of the stretching vibration of C = O and amide linkage –CONH [46]. The absorption peak at 1460 cm−1 is ascribed to C = C at 1460 cm−1, a peak at 1330 cm−1 is ascribed to C-C bond and a peak at 1035 cm−1 is assigned to C-O bond [47].

Further, to explore the bonding, surface state and chemical composition with in CQDs, XPS measurements were carried out. A wide full XPS survey spectrum exhibiting the binding energy of C1s, O1s and N1s at 284.50 eV, 399.9 eV, and 531.9 eV respectively is shown in Fig. 2 (a). This result revealed that carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulphur are the main elements of as-synthesized CQDs [48,49]. Also, high resolution XPS spectra of C1s show the peaks at 284.5 eV (C-N), 283.5 eV (C = C/C-C) and 285.8 eV (C = N/C = O) as shown in Fig. 2(b) [50]. The N1s spectra show the peaks at 399.9 eV (C-N-C) and 401.8 eV (N-H) as shown in Fig. 2(c) [51]. The O1s high resolution spectra as shown in Fig. 2(d), display three peaks at 532.7 eV (O-H/C-O-C) and 533.5 eV (C = O) [52]. These results confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl functional groups on CQDs surface which is in agreement with the results obtained from FTIR.

For studying the optical properties of the CQDs, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence studies were accomplished in detail. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), existence of the broad absorption peak in the range 250–290 nm is observed which corresponds to the n-p* transition of the carbonyl [42]. From an inset photograph as shown in Fig. 3(a), it is seen that the well dispersed aqueous suspension of CQDs displayed a light yellow colour under the normal visible light but under the UV lamp of wavelength 365 nm, it showed bright blue luminescence. The bright blue fluorescence emission was further investigated by CIE (Commission International De L’Eclairage) at co-ordinates (0.17,0.14) as shown in Fig. 3(b).

Fig. 3(c) demonstrated the excitation dependent fluorescence emission of the as-synthesized CQDs ranged from 280 nm to 420 nm. The emission spectra showed strong emission intensity under the excitation of 340 nm at 420 nm. Emission peak remain unchanged but intensity changes at an excitation from 280 to 340 nm. Further, emission peak shifts in a direction of longer wavelength at an excitation from 360 nm to 420 nm with progressive reduction in intensity. The excitation dependent fluorescence of CQDs may be because of several reasons. Firstly, FTIR spectra shows the presence of functional groups like –OH and –COOH on the surface of CQDs which confirmed abundant trap states and inhomogeneous chemical structure. These functional groups and emissive traps are basically responsible for fluorescence of CQDs and the excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour is because of the fact that certain emissive sites can be excited at particular wavelength [53]. Secondly, excitation dependent emission behaviour in nanostructures is due to discreteness of photon density of states [54]. Further, the most commonly accepted phenomenon responsible for the excitation dependent behaviour is the quantum confinement effect [55]. The mechanism for blue fluorescence in CQDs is still debatable. From the previous literature reported, it is seen that bandgap arises in isolated sp2 clusters whose size is less than 10 nm because of electron hole pair localization which is in accordance with blue fluorescence [56]. Thus, the doping with nitrogen atom having electron withdrawing nature plays an important role in yielding a blue fluorescence emission in the as synthesized CQDs [57]. Also, the quantum yield of the as-synthesized CQDs was found to be 22.7% under an excitation of 340 nm using rhodamine B as reference compound (QY = 31% in water). The comparison of synthesis method, size, optical properties and quantum yield with the earlier literature reports were presented in Table1
[58-62].

Further, the quenching behaviour of CQDs was studied using time dependent fluorescence lifetime spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. For the decay curve, the tri-exponential fit expression is specified by Eq. (2) [63]
(2)
N
t
=
B 1
e x p
(
- t
τ 1
)
+
B 2
e x p
(
- t
τ 2
)
+
B 3
e x p
(
- t
τ 3
)
where different decay times are denoted as τ1, τ2, τ3 and B1, B2, B3 represents corresponding amplitudes.

Fig. 3(d) displays decay curves of as-synthesized CQDs and after addition of Fe3+ ion in CQDs. Calculated values of lifetimes and average lifetimes (τavg) are shown in Table 2 . In order to calculate the average lifetime, following equation was used
(3)
τ
a v g
=
B 1
τ
1
2
+
B 2
τ
2
2
+
B 3
τ
3
2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3

From Eq. (3), the average lifetime of as-synthesized CQDs and after mixing 1 mM Fe3+ in CQDs was estimated to be 3.35 ns and 3.30 ns respectively having appreciable variations. Since the fluorescence lifetime is independent of CQDs concentration, so after chelation this is unaffected by the low concentration of CQDs. This means that fluorescence lifetime remains constant which confirms the static quenching mechanism [64].

The stability of the as-prepared CQDs was studied under various conditions. Under different ionic strength (0 to 2 M concentration) and with different pH values (3 to 10), the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs were investigated. The influence of ionic strength on relative intensity of CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 (a). Here F0 is the intensity of the blank sample and F is the fluorescence intensity after an incorporation of external substance, which is different ionic concentration in this case. It is clearly seen that intensity does not significantly change under high ionic concentration. This concludes that the as-prepared CQDs remain unvaried under high ionic conditions. To explore the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, different concentrations of pH (3 to 10) was added. Fig. 4(b) shows that fluorescence intensity showed a slight change on the addition of different pH values. Hence the present sensing system is slightly sensitive towards pH and eventually throughout the whole experiment, we have taken pH 6. Further, the as-prepared CQDs was incubated for one month and at different intervals, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. Fig. 4(c) shows that with in an incubation period, no remarkable change in intensity is observed.

With the aim of scrutinizing the sensing performance of the as-prepared CQDs, selectivity is an essential parameter to be investigated. For this, a change in fluorescence intensity in the presence of various metal ions have been analyzed. The concentration of 100 μL of the as-prepared CQDs was maintained in 1 ml of Na-Ac buffer (0.2 M, pH7). Afterwards, FL emission spectra were recorded by adding 100 μL of the different metal ions solution like Bi2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Sr2+, Na2+, Zn2+, Al3+ ions (500 μM) into it under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. From the spectra, fluorescence quenching is observed on adding Fe3+ ion (500 μM) while other metal ions show only a slight change as compared to blank which is almost negligible. Fig. 5 (a) shows the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) on addition of different metal ions where F is the fluorescence emission intensity as a result of incorporation of different metal ions and F0 is the fluoresecence intensity of CQDs. From the Fig. 5(a), it is clearly seen that the present sensing system is highly specific for selecting Fe3+ ion.

The sensing mechanism of CQDs is based on fluorescence quenching process as shown in Fig. 6 . The fast electron transfer between Fe3+ and oxygen containing groups on the surface of CQDs lead to fluorescence quenching effect [65]. Compared to other cations, oxygen containing groups like –OH, –COOH have excellent binding affinity towards Fe3+. Thus coordination interaction takes place which results electron transfer between excited state of CQDs and unfilled orbit of Fe3+and hence lead to fluorescence quenching [66]. Although other cations like Ni2+, Cu2+ can also bind with electron donating groups but due to their weaker electrophillic ability as compared with Fe3+, their fluorescence quenching is lower than that of Fe3+. Furthermore, fluorescence lifetime decay experiment confirms that quenching process is static quenching.

For examining the sensitivity of as-synthesized CQDs, different concentration of Fe3+ were incorporated in the CQD solution. With an increasing concentration of Fe3+ (0–500 μM), the effect on the intensity of as-synthesized CQDs were investigated at an excitation wavelength 360 nm at pH 6. Fig. 5(b) shows that the emission at 432 nm gradually decreases on increasing concentration of Fe3+, which illustrates that sensor is sensitive towards Fe3+. A good linear relation is confirmed in the range of 0–50 μM by investigating relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of Fe3+ as indicated in Fig. 7 (a) and Fig. 7(b). The following expression was used to determine the fluorescence quenching efficiency [37].
(4)
(
F O
F
)
- 1 = 0.0078
F e
3 +
+ 0.0051

Eq. (4) reveals linearity in the range of 0–50 μM with correlation factor R2 = 0.996. From the expression LOD = 3σ/k [67], we calculated the limit of detection (LOD), where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of regression line. The LOD estimated to be 0.2 μM which is compared with most of the LODs reported in literature [68-74] and can be seen from the data collected in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To exploit the relevancy of as-synthesized CQDs fluorescent probe sensors, standard addition method was carried out in real water samples which were taken from our laboratory’s tap water. The water samples were filtered using 0.22 μM syringe filter. As no iron is spotted in the tap water, thus a recovery experiment was performed in the water sample after spiking with standard concentration of Fe3+ (5,10,15 μM). With the concentration of Fe3+ added as demonstrated in Table 4 , resulting values are in accordance with recovery (%) which is in range (103.33–105) %. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 5% for all the three measurements which confirms its high feasibility and reproducibility and the results are in good agreement with those of the previous work reported [75,76].

In this work, we have successfully prepared CQDs by one step hydrothermal treatment of gelatin from a truly greener method without any chemical modification. The as-prepared CQDs has a quantum yield of 22.7% and exhibit blue fluorescence Furthermore, the as-prepared CQDs are found to be a good fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ion with high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection limit was found to be 0.2 μM in the linear range from 0 to 50 μM. Finally, this sensor was tested in real water samples for the detection of Fe3+ and obtained acceptable recoveries (103.33%-105%). The proposed sensor not only provided a promising CQDs fluorescent probes for the Fe3+ detection, but also exhibit a new approach to explore the gelatin for practical applications.
Urosa Latief: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing. Shafi ul Islam: Methodology. Zubair M.S.H. Khan: Conceptualization. Mohd. Shahid Khan: Conceptualization, Writing and Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to Central Instrumental Facility (CIF), Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi for providing research infrastructure for various characterization facilities.

Org. Lett.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Chem. 42 (2018) 14751-14756.
